name: Fermat Number Compositeness Theorem

phase_0.boundary_condition: The closed mathematical system where every Fermat number F_n = 2^(2^n) + 1 is either prime or composite, and compositeness is decidable via divisibility by smaller primes or explicit factorization.

VINIT.justification: Before any test or computation, F_n exists as a bare integer with no known factorization status — void state, no properties assigned.

TANCH.justification: Terminal anchor: every integer > 1 is either prime or a unique product of primes. This boundary contains and closes the entire compositeness question.

AFWD.justification: Forward morphism: systematic ascent through potential prime factors p < √(F_n), moving toward discovery of a divisor.

AREV.justification: Reverse morphism: descent when a candidate divisor fails, retracing to try the next candidate or conclude primality.

CLINK.justification: Sequential chaining: each divisor test is composed with the next, forming a pipeline of checks that collectively determine compositeness.

IMSCRIB.chosen_element: the identity property of F_n itself
IMSCRIB.justification: Self-reference: F_n is recognized as itself throughout all transformations; the number's intrinsic form is preserved as the invariant.

FSPLIT.justification: One input (F_n under test) splits into two branches: T-branch (divisor found → composite) and F-branch (no divisor found → prime or continue search).

FFUSE.justification: Both branches (divisor found or exhaustive search complete) fuse back into a single verdict: F_n is composite (if divisor exists) or prime (if none found). FFUSE(FSPLIT(F_n)) = compositeness_status(F_n).

EVALT.justification: Affirmative evaluation: T-branch succeeds when a prime factor p divides F_n, proving compositeness.

EVALF.chosen_element: no divisor found in range — primality candidate
EVALF.justification: Negative evaluation: F-branch succeeds when trial division exhausts all candidates up to √(F_n) without finding a factor.

ENGAGR.chosen_element: Fermat's own conjecture (all F_n are prime) vs. Euler's counterexample (F_5 = 4294967297 = 641 × 6700417)
ENGAGR.justification: Paradice: both states held simultaneously in mathematical history — the conjecture and its refutation coexist as a foundational tension in number theory, unresolved until explicit factorization.

IFIX.justification: Permanent, append-only record: once a divisor is found or primality is certified (e.g., via Lucas-Lehmer or other proof), the result is fixed and cannot be undone.

split_outputs: T-branch: divisor p found such that p | F_n
F-branch: no divisor found in trial range, or search continues
phase_2.fuse_result: compositeness_status(F_n) — a single boolean or certificate stating whether F_n is composite or prime

phase_3.void_description: 00 — F_n is uninitialized, no divisor tests performed, no factorization known
phase_3.true_description: 01 — F_n is composite; at least one prime divisor p has been found and verified
phase_3.false_description: 10 — F_n is prime; exhaustive trial division or primality proof has ruled out all smaller factors
phase_3.both_description: 11 — paradice state: F_n's compositeness is simultaneously conjectured and refuted (Fermat's conjecture vs. Euler's counterexample); both states held in mathematical tension until explicit computation resolves it

steps.domain_action: Initialize F_n as an uncomputed Fermat number 2^(2^n) + 1 with no known factorization.
Invoke the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic as the boundary that guarantees every F_n is either prime or composite.
Recognize F_n as itself — the invariant integer form that persists through all divisibility tests.
Begin trial division, ascending through candidate primes p = 3, 5, 7, 11, ... up to √(F_n).
Compose the first divisor test (p = 3) with the next (p = 5), forming a pipeline.
Compose the second test with the third, extending the pipeline.
Continue composing divisor tests sequentially until a divisor is found or the range is exhausted.
Branch on the outcome of the divisor search: does a prime p divide F_n?
T-branch activated — a divisor p has been found; F_n is composite.
Record the factorization F_n = p × q (or the explicit factors) as a permanent, irreversible certificate.
T-branch result (divisor found) fuses back into the verdict: F_n is composite.
F-branch activated — no divisor found in the trial range; descent into primality reasoning.
F-branch evaluation — no factor discovered; F_n is a prime candidate.
Record the primality certificate (or exhaustive search completion) as a permanent record.
F-branch result (no divisor found) fuses back into the verdict: F_n is prime.
Paradice state invoked — Fermat's conjecture (all F_n prime) and Euler's refutation (F_5 composite) are held simultaneously, representing the historical tension in the domain.
Self-reference loop: the compositeness status of F_n is recognized as the identity property of F_n itself.
Compose the final verdict with the next Fermat number F_(n+1), extending the algorithm to the entire sequence.
Close the system: the Fundamental Theorem guarantees that every Fermat number's compositeness is decidable and final.

phase_5.compiler_frontend: Trial division algorithm (or more sophisticated factorization methods like Pollard's rho, elliptic curve factorization) that translates the compositeness question into a sequence of divisibility tests.
phase_5.ipc_mechanism: Divisor candidates are passed from one test to the next; success/failure signals propagate up the pipeline to the FSPLIT/FFUSE junction.
phase_5.memory_mechanism: State is stored as (F_n, current_candidate_divisor, found_factor_flag). Once IFIX is reached, the factorization or primality certificate is appended to an immutable log.
phase_5.scheduler_mechanism: Sequential: each divisor test completes before the next begins. Parallelization is possible (test multiple candidates in parallel), but the logical order is preserved.
phase_5.alfs_store: The canonical reference is the sequence of known Fermat numbers and their factorizations: F_0 = 3 (prime), F_1 = 5 (prime), F_2 = 17 (prime), F_3 = 257 (prime), F_4 = 65537 (prime), F_5 = 4294967297 = 641 × 6700417 (composite), and all F_n for n ≥ 5 known to be composite.

phase_6.cycle_cost: Each divisor test consumes one modular exponentiation or division operation; total cost is O(√(F_n)) in the worst case (primality), or O(log(F_n)) if a small factor is found early.
phase_6.pre_cycle_state: F_n is uninitialized; no divisor tests have been performed; the system is in void state (00).
phase_6.post_cycle_state: F_n's compositeness status is determined and recorded; the system is in true state (01, composite) or false state (10, prime), or paradice state (11, if the historical tension is preserved).
phase_6.delta_s_verdict: ΔS ≈ 0 — The cycle is entropy-conservative because the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic is deterministic and reversible in principle: given the factorization, one can verify it; given the number, one can compute the factorization (though computationally hard). The information content of F_n is preserved; only its factorization status is revealed, not created.
